data.title: Fair Isaac's Fair Value Slips By 15%
data.description:
Full throttle mode. This describes the market we have seen since the early April lows. And yet we may forget that some stocks are losing investors' favor and are left behind. After all, we know that the main catalyst is currently AI spending. If you take a look at the YTD chart below, you can easily see that companies such as Mastercard (MA) and Visa (V), S&P Global (SPGI) and Moody's (MCO), as well as Equifax (EFX) and TransUnion (TRU), all the way down to Fair Isaac Corporation (NYSE:FICO), have underperformed the market, sometimes by a wide margin. If you have followed the news, you probably can at least say that there are some negative catalysts for all of these: stablecoins for the duopoly in digital payments, weaker financial data demand for the credit rating agencies, and regulatory shifts within credit scores and bureaus.

Although the companies I mentioned are different, they all share some similarities. They all operate in the financial systems and are positioned at the gateways of important crossroads. Many call them tollbooth stocks, highlighting that they have a business through which the whole traffic of the financial system has to pass, paying a fee to do so. Given this characteristic, they are all part of oligopolies or, in Fair Isaac's case (at least, until the last developments), monopolies. As financial toolbooth companies, they all have a capital-light structure and benefit from terrific margins generated by their scale and their extremely low cost of service replication. No wonder that investors usually bid these stocks high, often awarding earnings multiples in the high 20s or 30s. We have even seen FICO trade at a 50+ forward P/E.

They are also sensitive to the macrocycle. For example, SPGI and MCO saw their revenues decline when interest rates rose, as bond issuance softened. Rising delinquencies can also create a problem for FICO. Stressed consumers imply that spending softens, and digital payments see lower volume. But when we state these risks, we also understand how these setbacks are clearly temporary, as all of these companies thrive on key pillars of today's economy that can't be simply wiped away.

As tempting as it is to wrap up one article putting together all the data from these interesting companies, I decided to dedicate an article to Fair Isaac Corporation, as I think it is better to focus on one single company and assess it considering the broader picture. In fact, I wanted to revisit my "Strong Buy" thesis issued in May to see whether it still makes sense or not.

Fico: Quick Recap Of The Current Situation

Fair Isaac is widely known for its proprietary FICO score, which assesses the credit risk of a customer who is requesting a loan. The company also has a software business offering decision management solutions. But this business has grown more or less in line with inflation, while the true golden egg goose was the scores business. We see it clearly as we look at the past five quarters, with scores increasing 34% YoY.

Here, FICO was the undisputed leader, as it issued the only credit score that Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac accepted for mortgage underwriting. Since around 50% of all U.S. mortgages are sold to these two companies, we understand how wide FICO's moat was. Thanks to this position, Fair Isaac flexed aggressively its pricing power, increasing the price of a single FICO score from $0.50 in 2018 to almost $5.00 now. This is a CAGR close to 47%, which is not justified by a related cost increase. Lenders pay a fee every time they use a FICO score. Considering that for each mortgage three scores are required (one from each of the three credit bureaus), FICO gets three scores for each mortgage.

Fico often informed us that its growing revenues were coming from price hikes, and this eventually made regulators, in particular, the Federal Housing Finance Agency (FHFA), criticize this behavior, judging it unfair. The FHFA Director Bill Pulte announced that mortgage lenders can now accept the VantageScore 4.0 after saying that FICO has "ripped off Americans for decades." While we all understand that paying $5.00 to get a $400,000 loan is almost invisible and can't really be considered robbery, it is true that FICO flexed its pricing power too boldly at the cost of losing its monopolistic dominance. At the same time, FICO has also had to increase its lobbying expenditures, taking a double hit on capped revenue growth and increased cost structure. True, FICO owns around 90% of the credit-scoring market, but the introduction of the VantageScore creates a more uncertain environment for the company for two reasons.

First, FICO now faces a competitor that is accepted by the regulator and that will probably be more affordable (FICO's credit score margins hover between 85% and 90%, leaving ample room for the VantageScore to undercut FICO's prices while still earning money handsomely). Secondly, which seems to me the bigger threat, the VantageScore is owned by the three credit bureaus - Equifax, Experian, and TransUnion - that are FICO's data suppliers. Therefore, the VantageScore has a competitive edge: it doesn't have to pay to access the data. Moreover, the VantageScore also includes utility and rent payments, which makes it more complete. On the other hand, FICO benefits from strong network effects and the fine-tuning of its algorithms, which make its credit scoring so reliable.

Implications Of The New Regulatory Environment

When a company is a monopoly and then faces competition, it will lose at least a tiny part of its market share. This, per se, could not be a problem, but it surely implies that the company has lost a bit of its pricing power, especially in the case where the product or the service provided can be perfectly substituted by the new competitor's.

I don't expect FICO to raise prices next year, or, if it does so, they will be aligned with inflation. However, I think the biggest argument in FICO's favor right now is that the absolute value of its credit score price vs. the amount of the loans the consumers demand is still invisible. As a result, consumers won't put too much pressure on lenders and mortgage originators because of this fee. Usually, FICO is paid by credit bureaus, which are the FICO score distributors to the lenders. As distributors, they earn a credit report fee, a part of which is then remitted to Fair Isaac. So, the relationship with its suppliers and distributors is quite entangled and positions Fair Isaac in a tough spot right now. But since the cost of the FICO score is then passed onto the end customer, I think FICO's network effects will play a big role in retaining hold of most of its market share. The only incentive could come from VantageScore if it truly lowers prices and becomes a much better deal at scale for the lenders who could keep their lending cost structure the same (customers won't see if they are paying $3 or $5 for each of their scores) while increasing their profits. In any case, I don't expect consumers to benefit widely from the new regulatory environment. However, I believe Fair Isaac's growth will soften unless interest rates move sensibly down, sparking new demand for mortgages.

Since each mortgage generates three fee payments, a big variable that could offset FICO's slower growth is the growth rate of mortgage originations.

Fannie Mae released its September 2025 Housing Forecast and expects total home sales to rebound by 9.2% in 2026, with mortgage rates expected to move below 6% by the end of next year. At the same time, new mortgage originations are projected at $2.26 trillion for 2026, while a previous forecast expected $2.34 trillion. This is because, although home sales should increase, the supply remains tight and won't be as large as previously expected. Rising inflation also looms over this forecast, potentially impacting the rate cut cycle. At the same time, with interest rates moving down, those borrowers who took out a mortgage in the past three years are now incentivized to refinance it, leading to a likely increase in mortgage demand. We learned that in the two weeks around the day when the last rate cut was announced, the volume of mortgage applications in the U.S. rose by 30% YoY. But this was mostly led by refinancing demand rather than mortgages to purchase a new home.

FICO's Valuation

So far, the main takeaway should be: FICO will still do well, but it will probably grow at a slower pace and see lower profitability metrics. A positive catalyst could come from AI. FICO's expense structure sees personnel as the highest cost. In a business where algorithms and data play a key role, many jobs could be automated and replaced by AI agents.

We have not yet talked about FICO's FCF, but from the description we have made, we can clearly understand that the company is a cash flow generation machine. The TTM FCF is $747.9 million.

This is strong, but it also leads us to an issue. As we can see, FICO has bought back heavily its shares, with two big spikes after the pandemic. This was done by taking on debt. Currently, FICO carries around $2.4 billion in LT debt, which leads to a 2.67 debt/EBITDA ratio. It is not too high, but it also indicates that FICO can't raise more debt to fund massive buybacks. So, we should expect a bit more pressure on the stock as this important support will be tampered with.

In my past article, the stock had a market cap of $39 billion. I used a 12% FCF growth rate for a decade and a perpetual growth rate of 5%. Using a discount rate of 8%, I came to the conclusion that Fair Isaac was trading around its fair value, which seemed to be an opportunity to jump aboard.

Currently, I understand that the environment has changed and has become more challenging for FICO. As a result, I changed my inputs, lowering the growth rate of its FCF to 11% and reducing the time from 10 years to five years. The perpetual growth rate has also been lowered to 4%. The expected FCF for this year is around $850 million, which could be a bit generous. In any case, no matter how I want to twist it, right now FICO doesn't trade at fair value anymore but has reduced its intrinsic value by a fair amount, and I see its fair price around $1,300. This is a 15% drop from today's prices. Therefore, I am changing my rating to a sell, suggesting that FICO doesn't offer the guaranteed returns it did until recently. My idea is to swap my FICO shares for Equifax's or TransUnion's. But this would lead us astray, and we will deal with this idea in a future article.

data.title: InfraCap MLP ETF Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Returns in the midstream master limited partnership (MLP) sector underperformed the broader stock market in the second quarter of 2025. During the quarter, the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index (Benchmark Index) was down 4.65%, while the S&P 500® Index was up 10.94%. Additionally, energy commodities were lower in 2Q, with West Texas Intermediate crude prices down 8.91%, natural gas liquids down 19.03%, and natural gas (12-month average forward prices) down 12.67%.

During the quarter, natural gas distribution companies outperformed the broader midstream market, as price-sensitive companies felt the negative impact from commodity price declines. Over the last 18 months, natural gas distribution companies outperformed other midstream companies on the potential for growing power demand from data centers. We believe this greater demand will increase the value of storage and pipeline distribution networks, and that William Cos. (WMB), Kinder Morgan (KMI), TC Energy Corp. (TRP), and DT Midstream (DTM) may be well positioned to benefit from this trend. While the growth potential from data center power demand is still at an early stage, these companies have indicated ongoing conversations with counterparties that we believe will benefit them once formalized and announced in 2025.

On April 2, Trump announced tariffs that were far above market expectations, negatively impacting equities and commodities. Since then, Trump has delayed and softened his stance, leading to a significant market rally. The S&P 500 Index has now surpassed its pre-tariff announcement level. Other dominant market considerations are inflation and the U.S. Federal Reserve’s (Fed’s) decisions on short-term interest rates. The Fed acknowledged that meaningful progress toward lowering inflation occurred, and we expect upcoming data releases to confirm that inflation has moderated. Importantly, midstream companies may benefit from pipeline contracts linked to inflation, so they are naturally hedged against a higher inflation scenario. Therefore, midstream could perform well in both a soft-landing environment with low inflation and a higher-inflation-for-longer environment.

How the fund performed

During the quarter, the Fund returned -6.79% at net asset value (NAV). This compares to a return of -4.65% for the Benchmark Index. Relative to the Benchmark Index, the Fund’s leverage and interest expense detracted 1.20%, security selection and call writing added 0.56%, and fees and taxes detracted 1.49%. The Fund was negatively impacted by a 1.25% tax expense.

The Fund paid a monthly dividend of $0.29 per share for the last three distributions. NAV per share at quarter-end was $43.23 and the Fund’s 30-day SEC yield1 was 8.21%.

Top Contributors

  • Genesis Energy Partners (GEL) recently sold its soda ash business and reduced leverage.
  • Williams Cos. (WMB) is benefiting from additional power demand from data centers.
  • Cheniere Energy Inc. (LNG) reported earnings that were 14.3% better than consensus expectations.
  • Kinder Morgan (KMI) is benefiting from additional power demand from data centers.
  • Marathon Petroleum Corp. (MPC) benefited from higher forward crack spreads (refining margins).

Bottom Contributors

  • Plains All American (PAA) has been impacted by producers reducing rig counts in the Permian Basin.
  • Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) reported earnings that were 4.3% below expectations.
  • Sunoco LP (SUN) put out 2025 financial guidance that was slightly below consensus.
  • Cheniere Energy Partners (CQP) reported earnings and guidance that were in line with expectations.
  • MPLX LP (MPLX) continues to provide some of the most stable cashflows in midstream.

Portfolio Changes

During the quarter, we utilized proceeds to reallocate towards higher conviction names and took advantage of positive relative performance, while maintaining appropriate diversification.

Add: Hess Midstream Partners (HESM) has paused its secondary stock issuance, providing an entry point.

Add: Cheniere Energy Inc. (LNG) continues to be a top pick on relative value and growth.

Reduce: Venture Global (VG) is up by more than 50% since purchase.

Reduce: ONEOK (OKE) was moved to equal weight; production in the Bakken remains challenged.

Outlook

We believe MLPs, which were once widely recognized as a go-to vehicle for income-focused investors, can be a useful tool for investors looking for high income in a falling-interest-rate environment. Over the past few years, midstream companies successfully transitioned to positive free cash flow, sustainable dividends, and reasonable leverage levels. Earnings, which were historically low in the MLP space, are now substantially positive, and companies trade at reasonable price-to-earnings ratios of less than 12x. Additionally, debt-to-EBITDA, a measure of leverage, has fallen from an average of 4.5x to 3.6x. These developments support modest distribution growth, while facilitating the financing of capital expenditures from free cash flows. In 2025/2026, we expect midstream companies to continue transitioning to share buybacks and modest distribution growth.

We continue to monitor geopolitical tensions, OPEC+ (Organization of the Petroleum Supporting Companies) induced supply changes, and disciplined domestic exploration and production (E&P) budgets. In March 2025, Saudi Arabia announced it was increasing oil production earlier than originally expected, due to noncompliance by other OPEC+ member countries. Due to the additional production from Saudi Arabia, and tariff impacts on demand, we are now forecasting $60–$80 crude oil in 2025/2026. As an offset, the U.S. rig count has fallen by more than 7% year-to-date, which will reduce supply. Still, we continue to view midstream favorably, as E&P companies remain disciplined, and midstream benefits from liquefied natural gas export opportunities.

data.title: Navigating Brazil's Currency Volatility With USD Credit
data.description:
By Sally Wang

Brazil’s fixed income market is one of the deepest in emerging markets, with an outstanding government debt of over BRL 8 trillion (roughly 75% of GDP) and a corporate bond market dominated by floating-rate instruments tied to the interbank deposit certificate (CDI).

Domestic market participants are accustomed to high nominal yields; local treasury bills linked to the Selic often offer double-digit returns, creating a strong home-market bias. However, attractive as these base rates may appear to some market participants, they are not a substitute for diversified credit exposure. For institutional allocators, USD-denominated corporate bonds can provide access to global issuers, industry sectors and credit premium not fully represented in Brazil.

The challenge, however, is that unhedged USD credit exposure tends to come with significant FX volatility - in many cases, BRL moves can overwhelm the underlying credit performance. This tension between the richness of local carry and the diversification of global markets raises a central question for asset allocators: How can Brazilian market participants tap global credit markets while managing excessive currency risk?

Introducing the iBoxx BRL Hedged USD Credit Indices

To address this challenge, S&P Dow Jones Indices (S&P DJI) has introduced two new indices: the iBoxx USD Liquid Investment Grade BRL Hedge Carry Index (BRL) and the iBoxx USD Liquid High Yield BRL Hedge Carry Index (BRL).

Using a cash-bond-futures framework, the new indices seek to reduce currency volatility while preserving yield potential. To balance credit access with risk management, the iBoxx USD Liquid Investment Grade BRL Hedge Carry Index (BRL) has a 30% weight in the iBoxx $ Liquid Investment Grade Index (BRL) for core credit, has a 70% weight in cash earning the CETIP interbank rate to act as a volatility buffer and margin source and applies a -30% overlay in BRL futures via the S&P/B3 BRL-USD Mini Futures Index (BRL) ER to provide a structural hedge against currency risk.

From a Brazilian point of view, these indices offer a way to measure USD credit while addressing FX risk. By embedding a structural hedge, they have historically tended to show smoother BRL-adjusted performance that could integrate more naturally into local strategies. At the same time, the high local carry has helped to enhance yield potential, all while reducing overall volatility.

From a global perspective, this is a rules-based index that can help translate international credit market exposures into Brazilian market terms, leveraging the full capability of the S&P/B3 and iBoxx Fixed Income index Series.

Comparing the back-tested index level time series of the new iBoxx USD Liquid Investment Grade BRL Hedge Carry Index (BRL) with the iBoxx $ Liquid Investment Grade Index TR (BRL) shows that the new index often reduced volatility while maintaining competitive returns. There were also reduced drawdowns, where hedging mitigated the sharp FX-driven losses often seen in stress periods. A similar pattern was also observed in the iBoxx USD Liquid High Yield BRL Hedge Carry Index (BRL).

Exhibits 4 and 5 show that the iBoxx USD Liquid Investment Grade BRL Hedge Carry Index (BRL) saw volatility fall from 15.12% to 2.56% when compared to the benchmark, while the Sharpe ratio climbed from 0.75 to 3.61. For the iBoxx USD Liquid High Yield BRL Hedge Carry Index (BRL), volatility fell from 13.03% to 10.13%, while the Sharpe ratio improved modestly from 0.96 to 1.85, demonstrating an improvement on risk-adjusted return.

Part of a Broader Story: S&P DJI’s Brazilian Fixed Income Suite

These launches build on a decade of collaboration in Brazil. The new BRL hedged indices show the breadth of our capabilities, from sovereign debt to corporate credit, from local instruments to global assets framed in Brazilian terms. It is built on a decade of collaboration with B3 and experience in the Brazilian market.

This is not just another index suite - it’s part of a comprehensive toolkit that can help to empower issuers, allocators and individual market participants to navigate Brazil’s evolving financial landscape.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: NFL Eyes Bigger TV Bucks - Soonest
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

NFL Commissioner says the league is leaving money on the table. (0:15) New home sales with surprise surge in August. (2:20) Meet the autonomous commercial trucking disruptor. (2:57)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, NFL Commissioner Roger Goodell says the league could begin renegotiating terms for its television rights with media partners as soon as 2026, four years earlier than the current agreement’s opt-out clause.

Goodell said on CNBC the NFL is leaving money on the table when you look at other recent sports media deals.

To revisit terms, the league would need to convince its current media partners Disney (NYSE:DIS), NBCUniversal (NASDAQ:CMCSA), Paramount (NASDAQ:PSKY), Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) and Fox (NASDAQ:FOX) (FOXA) to start discussions on any new deal.

Back in 2021, the NFL entered into an $111 billion, 11-year rights deal that includes an opt-out clause after the 2029-30 season with all of its media partners except Disney, which has one extra year of rights.

"I think our partners would want to sit down and talk to us at any time, and we continue to dialogue with them. I like that opportunity," Goodell said. "Obviously it’s not going to happen this year. But it could happen as early as next year. That could happen."

"The reason why we felt so strongly about the option is the landscape is changing. It could be a long-term deal with the benefit of having that stability and security of it. But I think the reality of it is it changes so quickly that you want to have the ability to move. I think those options are going to give us a lot of flexibility to potentially go earlier," he said.

If the NFL manages to secure a new deal, it could also hike the salary cap in future seasons, giving teams more money to spend on players and expand rosters.

Among active stocks, Micron (MU) is lower following a sell-the-news reaction to better-than-expected quarterly results and guidance.

Morgan Stanley analyst Joseph Moore, who has an Equal-Weight rating on Micron and kept his $160 price target, said he believes the stock is “expensive” on book value, “extremely expensive” on free cash flow metrics, and “expensive” on cycle adjusted earnings.

Freeport-McMoRan (FCX) is slumping after guiding for Q3 consolidated sales to come in lower by 4% for copper and 6% for gold compared with its July forecast.

The company previously forecast consolidated sales of 1 billion lbs of copper and 350,000 oz of gold for the quarter.

And Acadia Healthcare (ACHC) is rallying after activist Engine Capital reported a 3% stake in the behavioral healthcare operator and is pushing for board changes and other moves.

Engine Capital is urging Acadia to halt capital-intensive growth projects, fix its core business and review its asset portfolio.

On the economic front, August new home sales jumped 20.5% to an annual rate of 800,000 from 664,000 in July. That was well ahead of the 650,000 consensus and up 15.4% from a year ago.

In other news of note, autonomous trucking startup firm Gatik continues to emerge as one of the leading companies in the self-driving truck space.

The company announced a significant expansion of its commercial partnership with Loblaw Companies (OTCPK:LBLCF) in a development that it said marks a milestone for the autonomous trucking industry.

Loblaw will deploy Gatik's autonomous fleet across its distribution network in the Greater Toronto Area. Under the initial phase of the expansion, 20 autonomous trucks equipped with Gatik's next-generation sensor suite will be deployed by the end of 2025, with a further 30 to be deployed by the end of 2026, creating a scalable platform for future growth phases. The autonomous commercial truck fleet could be one of the largest in North America.

The expanded fleet of Isuzu-built trucks will initially be deployed with safety drivers onboard before transitioning to freight-only (driverless) operations, delivering goods with greater frequency and responsiveness to more than 300 Loblaw stores.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, Goldman Sachs analysts see potential GDP growth accelerating over the next few years to above 2% -- thanks to AI.

They estimate that AI will drive productivity growth to 1.7% through the end of 2029, and then 1.9% in the early 2030s. This would lead to potential GDP growth in the 2.1-2.3% range.

Earlier this year, Goldman estimated that generative AI could boost global GDP by 7% or about $7 trillion, lifting productivity growth by 1.5 percentage points over the next 10 years.

Analysts have also estimated that AI has added $160 billion to what they deem as “true GDP,” which includes enterprise software adoption, productivity gains, and capex toward AI infrastructure, as well as private-sector AI investments and productivity boosts that are often excluded from traditional metrics.

data.title: Navitas Semiconductor: Impressive Design Wins Overly Priced In - Limited Return Prospects
data.description:
NVTS' Rally Occurred Overly Fast & Furious - Monetization Likely Delayed, Pending Conversion To Purchase Orders & Confirmed NVDA Partnership

We previously covered Navitas Semiconductor Corporation (NASDAQ:NVTS) in May 2025, discussing how its partnership with Nvidia (NVDA) had offered great long-term growth potential, albeit with minimal near-term financial impact, since Rubin Ultra would only be launched from 2027 onwards.

Despite the growing new lifetime design wins and pipeline, we had believed that its ongoing cash burn and deteriorating balance sheet were likely to trigger further dilutive capital raises, worsened by the risks arising from the drastically higher short interest volume and the overly expensive valuations.

NVTS 1Y Stock Price

Since that initial Hold rating, the bulls have proven our bearish thesis wrong indeed, with NVTS further rallying by +57.8% while well outperforming the wider market at +14.3% and its silicon-based power semiconductor peers in varying degrees.

Much of its tailwinds are naturally attributed to the exuberant sentiments surrounding the multi-year cloud super cycle, as numerous market leaders across the foundry, infrastructure, SaaS, REIT, and energy end markets highlight the durability of AI-related spending trends.

While some of the optimism appears to be warranted, as NVDA, the AI accelerator market leader, continues to guide double-digit FQ3 2026 (CQ3 2025) revenue growth, we believe that NVTS' rally has been overly done indeed.

This is because NVTS has been and may continue to report mixed sales cadence in the near term, attributed to the softer demand in the solar, industrial, and EV end markets, which consequently contributed to the impacted FQ2 2025 revenues of $14.49M (+3.4% QoQ/-29.1% YoY).

The management has already highlighted further sequential industrial weakness over the next few quarters as well, which perhaps explains the underwhelming FQ3 2025 revenue guidance of $10M (-30.9% QoQ/-53.9% YoY) and adj. gross margin guidance of 38.5% (in line QoQ/-1.2 YoY):

And then we've not seen ramp-ups of new design wins given the industry slowdowns continuing, I think, for a couple of quarters more. It's a little hard to predict even by our larger competitors. So I'd kind of give them equal weight on driving some of the sequential decline in Q3. (Seeking Alpha)

At the same time, the ongoing tariff headwinds are likely to be painful, since China (including Hong Kong) comprises 72% of NVTS' overall revenues in FQ2 2025 (-2 points YoY), worsened by the ongoing decline in the country's industrial/ manufacturing activities - with it likely to contribute to its lower sales trends ahead.

This is on top of the narrowing of its product portfolio as the management opts to transition away from the lower margin mobile products to data center-related/Gallium nitride/Silicon carbide technologies - with it underscoring why the recent stock price rally has not made sense, since the ongoing R&D efforts and the future commercialization cadence are likely to be a multi-year process.

Moving forward, despite the prior optimism surrounding the NVDA development collaboration, NVTS investors may want to temper their expectations indeed.

Our caution arises from the fact that NVDA has extended the collaboration invitation to NVTS' direct analog/SiC competitors, including ON Semiconductor Corporation (ON), STMicroelectronics N.V. (STM), Renesas Electronics Corporation (OTCPK:RNECF), and Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN), with it uncertain which company may eventually emerge as the winner.

The same has been highlighted by NVTS in the recent earnings call, based on the ongoing evaluation by Q4 2025, the final supplier selection/system design completion by 2026, and the volume production only expected in 2027 - with it exemplifying the highly speculative and competitive nature of the ongoing engagements with NVDA.

At the same time, we must highlight that out of NVTS' $2.4B of supposed pipeline (estimated potential future business), only $450M of design wins (selection of a Navitas product for a specific production program, stated in terms of revenues that may be realized over the life of the customer's end product) have been confirmed as of last quarter.

Given many of these are still in the "engineering sample" stages, it remains to be seen when these design wins may actually be realized as revenues, pending customer evaluation, purchase orders, mass production, and delivery.

Therefore, while NVTS may have previously highlighted 75 wins in data centers, 40 wins in EV e-mobility, 28 wins in appliance industrial, 100 wins in solar/energy storage, and 180 wins in mobile consumer in the FQ1'25 earnings call, we urge investors to closely monitor their announcements over the next few quarters, pending further clarification.

Otherwise, we believe that NVTS has already made the prudent choice to migrate some of its customers to its alternative foundry partner, Powerchip, attributed to the improved cost and capacity advantages, while potentially triggering lower prices and higher volumes.

Assuming that much of those design wins are converted into "purchase orders," we may see the company report a robust FY 2026 performance as well, before its data center monetization kicks in from FY 2026 onwards.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These developments perhaps explain why the consensus forward estimates remain mixed, with NVTS not expected to report meaningful top-line growth and breakeven performance in the near term.

The silver lining to NVTS' investment thesis will be the management's opportunistic capital raise at current inflated stock prices, as observed in the richer balance sheet at a net cash position of $161.18M (+114.5% QoQ/ +43.9% YoY).

This may allow the yet profitable company to bridge the ongoing cash burn at an annualized rate of -$42.24M (+10.6% QoQ/+20.6% YoY), albeit with further equity dilution as observed in the higher share count at 198.95M (+5.9% QoQ/+8.6% YoY).

NVTS Valuations

Given the uncertain top-line growth prospects at a consensus 3Y CAGR estimates of +4.5%, it goes without saying that NVTS is rather lofty at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 27.55x.

This is compared to the 1Y mean of 5.22x (prior to the NVDA announcement), the sector median of 3.50x, and its silicon-based power semiconductor peers, including:

  • ON at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 3.64x and a 3Y projected top-line growth at a CAGR of -0.3%,
  • STM at 1.91x/+3.8%,
  • RNECF at 3.39x/+5.1%,
  • Infineon Technologies AG (OTCQX:IFNNY) at 3.21x/+6.3%,
  • TXN at 9.73x/+10.9%,
  • Power Integrations, Inc. (POWI) at 4.69x/+13.4%, and
  • Wolfspeed, Inc. (WOLF) at 7.04x/+14.8%, respectively,

with it apparent that NVTS' uncertain growth prospects from the highly competitive NVDA collaboration and the inflated valuations do not offer a margin of safety here.

If anything, based on the rather elevated Enterprise Value of $1.37B by the time of writing (up from pre-NVDA partnership levels of $298.10M) and the consensus downgraded FY2027 revenue estimates from $154.49M at the start of the year to $94.95M by the time of writing, it is unsurprising that NVTS remains extremely expensive at our estimated FY 2027 EV/Sales valuations of 14.42x, with it underscoring its mixed return prospects at current heights.

Lastly, readers must also note the risks tied to single-client concentration if the NVDA partnership eventually works out, with it likely to trigger headwinds to NVTS' prospects if the former so chooses to diversify their supply sources and/or the AI bubble bursts before monetization kicks in by 2027.

So, Is NVTS Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

NVTS 4Y Stock Price

For now, NVTS has already regained part of its recent losses through the +29.2% stock price recovery from the September 2025 bottom, thanks to the overly done AI hype post Q2'25 earnings season, with the company's mixed fundamentals and the overly expensive valuations implying the stock's uncertain return prospects from current heights.

NVTS' Overbought Position

Thanks to the high double digits rally, it is unsurprising that NVTS is also overbought by the time of writing, with technical indicators and historical trends already implying a potential near-term correction, worsened by the drastically higher short interest volumes by +52.1% on a YoY basis.

Combined with the prolonged NVDA collaboration, the uncertain monetization of its design wins, and the potentially lumpy industrial demand recovery, we prefer to maintain our prior Hold rating here.

It may be more prudent to adopt a wait-and-see attitude in the meantime.

data.title: Virtus Newfleet Core Plus Bond Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fixed Income Market Review

The second quarter of 2025 was defined by several key policies from the new Trump administration, including tariffs/trade, immigration, and taxes. “Liberation Day” was marked by tariffs that were significantly higher than market expectations. This created a stretch of financial market volatility while economists and analysts recalibrated the potential economic implications. Market volatility was followed by policy implementation delays and legal challenges when the tariffs went into effect. Final tariff and tax policy outcomes remain unknown, and trade deals signed to date have been limited. However, financial markets gained confidence over the quarter when the economy seemed to remain solid and inflation continued to improve.

Despite the policy uncertainty, most U.S. data continued to point to a resilient economy. Growth was slowing, inflation was moderating, and unemployment remained low by historical standards. This allowed the Federal Reserve to maintain a patient easing bias while it awaited greater clarity on the impact of the new policies on the economy. Global monetary policy remains desynchronized, with divergences among the European Central Bank, the Bank of England, and the Bank of Japan. This, along with fiscal developments in Europe and the U.S., had meaningful foreign exchange impacts and resulted in a weaker U.S. dollar during the period. Geopolitical developments were decidedly mixed during the second quarter, with volatility re-emerging in the Middle East and little progress in the Russia/Ukraine conflict.

The weight of U.S. policy uncertainty lifted as the quarter progressed and resulted in more varied financial market performance. Fixed income sector performance was also variable, with interest rates mixed depending on tenor during the period. Most spread sectors outperformed U.S. Treasuries, and the U.S. Treasury curve steepened. The 2-year Treasury yield decreased 16 basis points (bps), the 5-year Treasury yield decreased by 13 bps, the 10-year Treasury yield increased by 6 bps, and the 30-year Treasury yield moved 25 bps higher.

Fund Performance Review

The Fund (Class I)(MUTF:SAVYX) returned 1.57% in the second quarter versus the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index return of 1.21%.

Positive Contributors

  • U.S. Treasury Securities: The Fund’s underweight to U.S. Treasuries had a positive impact, as most spread sectors outperformed U.S. Treasuries and had positive excess returns.
  • Investment Grade (IG) Corporates Selection: Selection within the sector was positive.
  • High Yield Corporates: Allocation to the sector was positive.

Performance Detractors

  • U.S. Treasury Curve Positioning: While the underweight to U.S. Treasuries had a positive impact on performance, curve positioning was a detractor.

Current Fund Strategy and Positioning

  • Increased exposure to agency mortgage-backed securities and IG corporates. The overall cash allocation was also higher.
  • Reduced exposure to bank loans and non-agency commercial mortgage-backed securities.
  • In addition to changes to the Fund’s sector allocation during the quarter, we continue to optimize positioning within sectors based on our view of the best relative value.

Emerging Market (EM) Debt and Non-U.S. Exposure: The return on the J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Diversified Index was favorable in 2Q at 3.29%, with EM high yield’s 4.28% outperforming EM IG’s 2.29% during the period. EM corporate debt returned 1.58%. During the period, Argentina was approved for a $20 billion IMF program, Ecuador re-elected Daniel Noboa, and Romania’s second-round presidential election saw the victory of centrist Nicușor Dan. Throughout the quarter, the overall EM exposure in the Fund was unchanged. The Fund maintained a preference for U.S. dollar assets and EM high yield over EM IG.

IG Corporates: IG corporate spreads began 2Q at 94 bps, surged to 119 bps after Liberation Day tariffs were announced, and subsequently rallied all the way back into the mid-80s by quarter-end. This positions spreads within 10 bps of the 20-year lows reached in late 2024. Yields at 4.99% are slightly lower sequentially, driven by this decline in spread. Heavy outflows at the start of the quarter gave way to consistent inflows, albeit lower than 1Q levels. Gross supply of $375 billion in the quarter is in line with the five-year average (ex-2020). We favor U.S. banks as their fundamentals have improved. Within industrials, we have focused on defensive and domestic-oriented industries such as utilities and pipelines.

High Yield Corporates: The high yield market, as represented by the Bloomberg High Yield 2% Issuer Capped Bond Index, gained 3.53% in 2Q. Credit spreads tightened 57 bps to +290 bps. Positive trade developments, prospects for rate cuts, easing geopolitical tensions, robust inflows at quarter-end, and supportive earnings contributed to the strong performance. Healthcare outperformed on prospects of more benign legislative treatment, while energy underperformed due to weaker oil prices.

Securitized: After the spread widening from Liberation Day tariff news, all markets settled down, and the securitized sector experienced some spread tightening as the quarter ended, with strong demand meeting a plethora of new issue volumes. This, coupled with lower U.S. Treasury rates, enabled securitized markets to post positive total and excess returns. Though our investable dollars mostly remain higher in the capital stack, we are selectively looking at individual transactions where we believe opportunities may exist lower in the capital stack.
